title: A translator's third stanza
summary: A translator brings a stuck poem to the poet who wrote it. He surprises her by telling her to break it — and by explaining, gently, what an honest translation actually is.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Bác Hải, I am rendering your poem 'October Doorway' into English. I have a problem I would like to ask you about — but I am embarrassed to.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Embarrassed? Why?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Because I think part of your poem cannot be translated, and I do not know what to do with it.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Sit down. We have an hour. Show me the part.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: The third stanza. The lines about the doorway in October. In Vietnamese, there is a single word — 'hanh' — which carries the cool late-autumn northern wind, the smell of dry leaves, the feeling of needing a thin scarf but not a coat. I have tried for two days. I have written 'crisp', 'brisk', 'autumnal'. None of them work.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: None of them will work.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: I was afraid of that.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Don't be afraid. It is what it is. English does not have hanh, just as Vietnamese does not have 'wistful'. We exchange one set of holes for another. The Russians lose all their cases coming into English. The Germans lose half their compound structure. The English lose, going into Vietnamese, the strange pleasure of stacking three modal verbs in a row. Every direction loses something. The pretence that translation is a transfer without loss is the first lie of the trade.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: But the poem turns on that word. If I lose hanh, I lose the doorway.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Then lose the doorway.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: I cannot lose the doorway. The whole poem is the doorway.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Then change the poem.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Bác. You are asking me to write a different poem under your name.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: I am asking you to write the English poem that the Vietnamese poem would have been if I had been born in Northumberland in 1955. That is the only honest translation. The pretence that the English-speaker can have my poem without writing a new poem with me is what cheapens translation everywhere.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Then what are you, in the new poem?

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: A ghost the translator borrows from.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: That is a generous way of putting it.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: It is also the truth. Now — what English word do you have for late October that is local enough to mean something? Not 'crisp'. Crisp is dead. Words die when they get tired. They get tired when too many writers use them lazily, for too many decades, until the inside of the word is hollowed out and only the shape remains. What do English-speaking northern people actually feel in October?

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: They talk about 'first proper coat' weather. The week when you put on the coat for the first time and feel grown-up again.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: There. Use that. Build the doorway out of the first-coat morning, not out of hanh. You are not lying to the English reader. You are telling them where the doorway is, in a city they can find.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: And the Vietnamese reader who reads my translation back into Vietnamese — what do they get?

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: A different poem. They should read the original. The translation is for the people who cannot.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: I never thought of it that way.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Most translators do not, and we punish them by giving them prizes for fidelity to the wrong thing. They are faithful to the dictionary. They are unfaithful to the doorway.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: What do I do with the title?

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Keep the title. Titles can travel. Doorways are doorways everywhere. The trouble is always in the third stanza.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: Thank you. I have one more question, if I may.

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: Always.

body.speaker: Linh
body.text: When I do this — when I rewrite the poem so it can live in English — am I a translator at all? Or am I a co-author who is being too modest about it?

body.speaker: Bác Hai
body.text: You are a translator. A co-author who is being too modest is what an honest translator is.

annotations.meaningVi: chuyển ngữ, dịch lại (đặc biệt là văn học)

grammarNotes.title: Hedged challenge: 'I am asking you to…' + 'That is the only…'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Ở C1, người nói thường dùng cấu trúc 'I am asking you to + V' để diễn đạt yêu cầu mạnh nhưng vẫn lịch sự; theo sau là một mệnh đề tuyên bố nguyên tắc bằng 'X is the only Y'. Trong đối thoại: 'I am asking you to write the English poem that the Vietnamese poem would have been if I had been born in Northumberland in 1955. That is the only honest translation.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: At C1, speakers often use 'I am asking you to + verb' to phrase a strong but polite request, followed by a principled claim with 'X is the only Y'. From the dialogue: 'I am asking you to write the English poem that the Vietnamese poem would have been if I had been born in Northumberland in 1955. That is the only honest translation.'

cloze.template:
Linh: Bác Hải, I am {{b1}} your poem 'October Doorway' into English. I have a problem I would like to ask you about — but I am embarrassed to.
Bác Hai: Embarrassed? Why?
Linh: Because I think part of your poem cannot be translated, and I do not know what to do with it.
Bác Hai: Sit down. We have an hour. Show me the part.
Linh: The third {{b2}}. The lines about the doorway in October. In Vietnamese, there is a single word — 'hanh' — which carries the cool late-autumn northern wind, the smell of dry leaves, the feeling of needing a thin {{b3}} but not a coat. I have tried for two days. I have written 'crisp', 'brisk', 'autumnal'. None of them work.
Bác Hai: None of them will work.
Linh: I was {{b4}} of that.
Bác Hai: Don't be afraid. It is what it is. English does not have hanh, just as Vietnamese does not have 'wistful'. We exchange one set of {{b5}} for another. The Russians lose all their cases coming into English. The Germans lose half their compound structure. The English lose, going into Vietnamese, the strange pleasure of stacking three modal verbs in a row. Every direction loses something. The pretence that translation is a transfer without loss is the first lie of the trade.
Linh: But the poem turns on that word. If I lose hanh, I lose the {{b6}}.
Bác Hai: Then lose the doorway.
Linh: I cannot lose the doorway. The whole poem is the doorway.
Bác Hai: Then change the poem.
Linh: Bác. You are asking me to write a different poem under your name.
Bác Hai: I am asking you to write the English poem that the Vietnamese poem would have been if I had been born in Northumberland in 1955. That is the only honest translation. The pretence that the English-speaker can have my poem without writing a new poem with me is what cheapens translation everywhere.
Linh: Then what are you, in the new poem?
Bác Hai: A {{b7}} the translator borrows from.
Linh: That is a generous way of putting it.
Bác Hai: It is also the truth. Now — what English word do you have for late October that is local enough to mean something? Not '{{b8}}'. Crisp is dead. Words die when they get tired. They get tired when too many writers use them lazily, for too many decades, until the inside of the word is hollowed out and only the shape remains. What do English-speaking northern people actually feel in October?
Linh: They talk about 'first proper {{b9}}' weather. The week when you put on the coat for the first time and feel grown-up again.
Bác Hai: There. Use that. Build the doorway out of the first-coat morning, not out of hanh. You are not {{b10}} to the English reader. You are telling them where the doorway is, in a city they can find.
Linh: And the Vietnamese reader who reads my translation back into Vietnamese — what do they get?
Bác Hai: A different poem. They should read the {{b11}}. The translation is for the people who cannot.
Linh: I never thought of it that way.
Bác Hai: Most translators do not, and we punish them by giving them prizes for {{b12}} to the wrong thing. They are faithful to the dictionary. They are unfaithful to the doorway.
Linh: What do I do with the {{b13}}?
Bác Hai: Keep the title. Titles can {{b14}}. Doorways are doorways everywhere. The trouble is always in the third stanza.
Linh: Thank you. I have one more question, if I may.
Bác Hai: Always.
Linh: When I do this — when I rewrite the poem so it can live in English — am I a translator at all? Or am I a {{b15}} who is being too modest about it?
Bác Hai: You are a translator. A co-author who is being too {{b16}} is what an honest translator is.

blanks.explanation: Rendering — translating (especially of literary work) — into English.
The third stanza is where the untranslatable word sits.
Needing a thin scarf but not a coat — the very specific feel of 'hanh'.
'We exchange one set of holes for another.' Every language has gaps; translation moves them around.
Without 'hanh', Linh fears she loses the doorway — the poem's central image.
'A ghost the translator borrows from.' The poet becomes a presence the translator works with.
Bác Hai rejects 'crisp' — 'Crisp is dead.' He wants a word that still has local meaning.
'First proper coat' weather — the English equivalent moment, located in northern speech.
'You are not lying to the English reader. You are telling them where the doorway is.'
Vietnamese readers should read the original. The translation is for those who cannot.
Prizes are given for fidelity to the wrong thing — the dictionary, not the doorway.
Linh asks what to do with the title — 'October Doorway'.
'Titles can travel.' Doorways are doorways everywhere — but the third stanza is where the work is.
Linh asks if she is really a co-author who is being too modest.
'A co-author who is being too modest is what an honest translator is.'

translationVi:
Linh: Bác Hải ơi, cháu đang dịch bài thơ 'Cửa Tháng Mười' của bác sang tiếng Anh. Cháu có một vấn đề muốn hỏi bác — nhưng cháu ngại ạ.
Bác Hai: Ngại? Sao thế?
Linh: Vì cháu nghĩ một phần bài thơ của bác không thể dịch được, và cháu không biết nên làm gì.
Bác Hai: Ngồi xuống. Mình có một giờ. Đưa bác xem chỗ đó.
Linh: Khổ thơ thứ ba. Những câu về cánh cửa vào tháng Mười. Trong tiếng Việt có một từ — 'hanh' — chứa cả ngọn gió cuối thu của miền Bắc, mùi của lá khô, cảm giác cần một chiếc khăn mỏng nhưng chưa cần áo khoác. Cháu đã thử suốt hai ngày. Cháu viết 'crisp', 'brisk', 'autumnal'. Không từ nào hợp cả.
Bác Hai: Không từ nào hợp đâu.
Linh: Cháu sợ là vậy.
Bác Hai: Đừng sợ. Là thế thôi. Tiếng Anh không có 'hanh', cũng như tiếng Việt không có 'wistful'. Chúng ta đổi bộ lỗ này lấy bộ lỗ khác.
Linh: Nhưng bài thơ xoay quanh từ ấy. Nếu cháu mất 'hanh', cháu mất cánh cửa.
Bác Hai: Vậy thì mất cánh cửa đi.
Linh: Cháu không thể mất cánh cửa. Cả bài thơ là cánh cửa.
Bác Hai: Vậy thì đổi bài thơ.
Linh: Bác. Bác đang bảo cháu viết một bài thơ khác dưới tên bác.
Bác Hai: Bác đang bảo cháu viết bài thơ tiếng Anh mà bài thơ tiếng Việt đáng lẽ đã trở thành nếu bác sinh ra ở Northumberland năm 1955. Đó là cách dịch trung thực duy nhất. Sự giả vờ rằng người nói tiếng Anh có thể có bài thơ của bác mà không cần cùng bác viết một bài thơ mới — đó là cái làm rẻ rúng nghề dịch ở khắp mọi nơi.
Linh: Vậy trong bài thơ mới, bác là gì?
Bác Hai: Một bóng ma mà người dịch mượn lại.
Linh: Bác nói thế thật rộng lượng.
Bác Hai: Cũng là sự thật. Giờ — cháu có từ tiếng Anh nào cho cuối tháng Mười đủ địa phương để có nghĩa không? Không phải 'crisp'. 'Crisp' chết rồi. Người miền bắc nói tiếng Anh thật sự cảm thấy gì vào tháng Mười?
Linh: Họ nói về 'first proper coat' weather. Cái tuần mà ta mặc áo khoác lần đầu và lại cảm thấy mình là người lớn.
Bác Hai: Đấy. Dùng cái đó. Dựng cánh cửa từ buổi sáng mặc áo khoác đầu tiên, chứ không phải từ 'hanh'. Cháu không nói dối người đọc tiếng Anh. Cháu chỉ chỉ cho họ cánh cửa nằm ở đâu, trong một thành phố mà họ có thể tìm thấy.
Linh: Và người đọc tiếng Việt đọc bản dịch của cháu rồi dịch ngược về tiếng Việt — họ nhận được gì?
Bác Hai: Một bài thơ khác. Họ nên đọc nguyên bản. Bản dịch dành cho những người không thể đọc nguyên bản.
Linh: Cháu chưa bao giờ nghĩ theo cách đó.
Bác Hai: Hầu hết người dịch không nghĩ như vậy, và chúng ta phạt họ bằng cách trao giải cho sự trung thành sai chỗ. Họ trung thành với từ điển. Họ phản bội cánh cửa.
Linh: Cháu làm gì với tựa đề ạ?
Bác Hai: Giữ tựa đề. Tựa đề có thể đi qua biên giới. Cánh cửa thì ở đâu cũng là cánh cửa. Rắc rối luôn ở khổ thơ thứ ba.
Linh: Cảm ơn bác. Cháu xin hỏi thêm một câu nữa, nếu được.
Bác Hai: Bất cứ lúc nào.
Linh: Khi cháu làm việc này — khi cháu viết lại bài thơ để nó có thể sống trong tiếng Anh — cháu có phải là người dịch không? Hay cháu là một đồng tác giả đang quá khiêm tốn về điều đó?
Bác Hai: Cháu là người dịch. Một đồng tác giả đang quá khiêm tốn — đó chính là người dịch trung thực.

questions.prompt: What is Linh embarrassed to admit to Bác Hai?
options: She believes part of his poem cannot be translated, and she does not know what to do
She wants to be paid more
questions.explanation: 'I think part of your poem cannot be translated, and I do not know what to do with it.'
questions.hint: Look at Linh's first long turn.

options: A single Vietnamese word, 'hanh', which carries the late-autumn northern wind, dry leaves, needing a thin scarf but not a coat
questions.explanation: Linh describes 'hanh' in her first long turn.
questions.hint: Look at Linh's description of the third stanza.

questions.prompt: What does Bác Hai say about 'crisp', 'brisk', and 'autumnal'?
options: None of them will work — and that is fine; English does not have hanh, just as Vietnamese does not have 'wistful'
questions.explanation: 'None of them will work.' / 'English does not have hanh, just as Vietnamese does not have wistful.'

questions.prompt: What metaphor does he use for the gaps between languages?
options: Holes — 'We exchange one set of holes for another'
questions.explanation: 'We exchange one set of holes for another.'

questions.prompt: When Linh says she cannot lose 'the doorway', what does Bác Hai tell her to do?
options: Change the poem — write the English poem the Vietnamese poem would have been if he had been born in Northumberland in 1955
questions.explanation: 'I am asking you to write the English poem that the Vietnamese poem would have been if I had been born in Northumberland in 1955.'
questions.hint: Look at his longest turn early in the dialogue.

questions.prompt: How does Bác Hai describe his own role in the translation?
options: A ghost the translator borrows from
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'Then what are you, in the new poem?'

questions.prompt: What English phrase does Linh offer as a local equivalent for 'hanh'?
questions.hint: Look at Linh's reply about late October.

questions.prompt: What does Bác Hai say about lying to the English reader by changing the word?
options: She is lying — and should stop translating
She is not lying — she is telling them where the doorway is, in a city they can find
questions.explanation: 'You are not lying to the English reader. You are telling them where the doorway is, in a city they can find.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn after Linh's 'first proper coat' phrase.

questions.prompt: What happens, in Bác Hai's view, if a Vietnamese reader reads the English translation back into Vietnamese?
options: They get a different poem — and they should read the original; translation is for people who cannot
questions.explanation: 'A different poem. They should read the original. The translation is for the people who cannot.'
questions.hint: Look at the exchange about the Vietnamese reader.

questions.prompt: Why does Bác Hai say translators win prizes for the wrong thing?
options: They are faithful to the dictionary but unfaithful to the doorway
Prizes go to the wealthy only
questions.explanation: 'They are faithful to the dictionary. They are unfaithful to the doorway.'
questions.hint: Look at his turn about prizes.

questions.prompt: What does Bác Hai say about the title 'October Doorway'?
options: Keep it — 'Titles can travel. Doorways are doorways everywhere. The trouble is always in the third stanza.'
Use a different word for October
questions.hint: Look at his reply to 'What do I do with the title?'

options: How much she will be paid
Whether she is a translator at all, or a co-author who is being too modest
Whether she can use his pen name
questions.explanation: 'Am I a translator at all? Or am I a co-author who is being too modest about it?'
questions.hint: Look at the very last question Linh asks.

questions.prompt: What is Bác Hai's answer to that last question?
options: She is a translator — and 'a co-author who is being too modest is what an honest translator is'
questions.explanation: 'You are a translator. A co-author who is being too modest is what an honest translator is.'
questions.hint: Look at the very last line.

questions.prompt: Which paraphrase is closer to Bác Hai's central view?
options: A faithful translation reproduces every word exactly.
An honest translation rewrites the poem so it can stand in the new language — fidelity to the doorway, not the dictionary.
Translation is impossible and should not be attempted.
Only the author should translate their own work.
questions.explanation: His repeated phrase 'fidelity to the doorway' sums this up.
questions.hint: Look at his turn about prizes and dictionaries.

questions.prompt: Which of these is Bác Hai NOT claiming?
options: That every language has gaps the other languages do not have
That the original word 'hanh' can be preserved if the translator tries harder
That titles travel more easily than third stanzas
questions.explanation: He explicitly says 'None of them will work' — no English word can preserve 'hanh'.
questions.hint: Look at his early reply about 'crisp', 'brisk', and 'autumnal'.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the conversation?
options: Generous and exacting — Bác Hai treats Linh as a peer, gives her permission to break the literal poem, and ends by naming her honestly
questions.explanation: Bác Hai calls Linh's framing 'a generous way of putting it' and grants her a strong, dignifying role at the end.
questions.hint: Notice how he handles her embarrassment and her last, scared question.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Bác Hai says an honest translator is 'a co-author who is being too modest'. Think of a time you carried someone else's meaning across — into another language, another generation, another field. Were you faithful to their words, or to what their words were doing? Did the receiving side gain more from the literal version, or from the version that landed?
